reasoning: The question asks if there will be >10x the baseline number of riots (which works out to ~48+ riots in a 30-day period) prior to each resolution date. I found that in the UK, reaching ~50 riots in a single month is extremely rare; it happened during extreme societal breakdown like the August 2024 far-right riots (and previously the 2011 London riots). For the near-term dates (April, May, July, Oct 2026), there is currently no evidence of massive ongoing rioting — while there have been large anti-racism and far-right marches in early 2026, they have mostly been categorized as protests, with isolated skirmishes rather than massive nationwide rioting. Therefore, the immediate dates map closely to the 1-5% baseline risk of a spontaneous nationwide riot breaking out. For further-out dates (2027 to 2036), the probability steadily increases to reflect fundamental uncertainty: the baseline could mechanically increase over time (e.g., if protests become more violent or ACLED methodology shifts), or there may be deep sociopolitical unrest in the 2030s. Still, reaching 10x the historical average is an inherently extreme event (unless the baseline first drops artificially low, although it's anchored to a minimum of 1), thus probabilities naturally remain low but climb to ~18% at the 10-year horizon.
